figures.caption: Figure 4: Compute–accuracy trade-off on the gemini-2.5-flash-001 adversarial split. CDD-α at τ = 0.7 routes 30% of samples through deep decomposition and reaches 68.5%; the remaining 9.6 pp gap to Full CDD costs roughly 1.4× more compute. Relative compute is approximate; exact ratios depend on token-level prompt and rationale lengths.

Figure 3: Mistake-injection causal sensitivity across model families (N=100 per cell). On Gemini-2.5Flash, CDD’s resolution trace is strongly coupled to the final answer (64.1%); on all three Claude variants the signal falls inside the shaded noise band, even though adversarial accuracy still improves under CDD (Table 2). The Claude-Sonnet Vanilla-CoT bar (20.3%) is an outlier discussed in §6.3.

Figure 2: Adversarial accuracy on the full Epi-Scale adversarial split (gemini-2.5-flash-001, N=2,250; ∼562–563 examples per perturbation; normal-approximation 95% binomial CIs). CDD scores 88.0%, 83.2%, 71.3%, and 69.9% across the four perturbation types, yielding the highest accuracy in each column and showing the strongest gains on explicit factual conflicts while remaining robust under temporal drift.

Table 1: Adversarial accuracy across perturbation types on the full Epi-Scale adversarial split (gemini-2.5-flash-001, N=2,250; ∼562–563 samples per perturbation; normal-approximation 95% binomial CIs). Macro Avg. is the unweighted arithmetic mean of the four perturbation accuracies; the rightmost interval propagates the four per-cell binomial uncertainties.

Figure 1: CDD pipeline with the CDD-α NLI-gated bypass. High-conflict samples enter the full decomposition probe, while low-conflict samples follow the Standard RAG bypass before converging at the final answer.
